items.question: State the naive Bayes conditional independence assumption and write the classification rule.
items.answer: Given y, P(x|y) = ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ|y). Predict ŷ = argmax_y P(y) ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ|y), equivalently argmax_y [log P(y) + Σᵢ log P(xᵢ|y)]. The P(x) denominator cancels in argmax.

items.question: Why is naive Bayes called "naive," and is the assumption about marginal or conditional independence?
items.answer: It assumes features are independent given the class label y (conditional independence), not marginally independent in the raw data. "Naive" reflects that real features often interact even after conditioning on y, yet the product approximation is still useful.

items.question: An email has FREE=1 and !!!=1. P(spam)=0.4, P(FREE=1|spam)=0.8, P(!!!=1|spam)=0.7, P(FREE=1|ham)=0.1, P(!!!=1|ham)=0.05. Which class wins?
items.answer: Spam score ∝ 0.4×0.8×0.7 = 0.224. Ham score ∝ 0.6×0.1×0.05 = 0.003. Spam wins by a large margin. Always include the prior P(y) in the product.

items.question: How does naive Bayes relate to Bayes' theorem, and where does independence enter?
items.answer: Bayes gives P(y|x) = P(x|y)P(y)/P(x). Independence enters only in modeling P(x|y) as ∏ P(xᵢ|y). Bayes' theorem itself requires no independence; the assumption makes the likelihood tractable.

items.question: What is the zero-frequency problem and how do you fix it?
items.answer: If a feature value never co-occurs with class y in training, P(xᵢ|y)=0 and the whole class likelihood becomes zero. Fix with Laplace smoothing: add α to counts in numerator and α·V to denominator so no probability is exactly zero.
